title: NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical-environmental resilience evidence
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel or competent-authority review. Does not execute restore tests or validate backup encryption—only monitors ingested status and environmental telemetry.
evidence: Daily saved search `UC-22.2.53` output, dashboard workbook, CSV export, and archive copy in `index=audit_evidence` with timestamped multisearch extracts.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(c) receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(c) using NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical-environmental resilience evidence.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search for NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical-environmental resilience evidence, dashboard panel, and archived result export in index=audit_evidence for Art.21(2)(c).

controlTest.positiveScenario: A Tier-1 database backup finishes jobs but `last_success_epoch` is older than the service’s `recovery_point_hours` while multisearch also shows a red ITSI KPI for the same `service_id`; the combined search returns a row listing backup_restore and service_health streams.
controlTest.negativeScenario: All Tier-1 backups succeed within the hour, door denials tie to guard drills with approved tickets, environmental sensors stay inside thresholds, and ITSI KPI tiles stay green—so the post-lookup filter yields zero rows when executed over the same test window.

dataSources: `index=backup` `sourcetype=backup:job_status` with extracted fields `job_name`, `job_status`, `bytes_transferred`, `duration_sec`, `last_success_epoch`. `index=physical` `sourcetype=access_control:door` with `reader_id`, `badge_id`, `access_result`, `datacenter_zone`. `index=environmental` (`sourcetype=sensor:temperature` or `sourcetype=sensor:humidity`) with `site_id`, `reading`, `threshold_status`. `index=itsi_summary` service health summaries keyed to critical CIs. Splunk ITSI ([Splunkbase 1841](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1841)), Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)). Lookup `critical_services.csv`: `service_id`, `service_name`, `owner`, `recovery_time_hours`, `recovery_point_hours`.
app: Splunk IT Service Intelligence (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)
spl:
| multisearch [
    search index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-7d (job_status="Failed" OR job_status="CompletedWithExceptions" OR isnull(last_success_epoch))
    | eval resilience_stream="backup_restore"
    | eval service_id=coalesce(service_tag, job_name, host, media_server)
    | eval detail=printf("backup_job=%s status=%s", job_name, job_status)
    search index=physical sourcetype=access_control:door earliest=-7d (access_result IN ("Denied","Forced","Tailgate") OR match(lower(event_subtype),"after_hours"))
    | eval resilience_stream="physical_security"
    | eval service_id=coalesce(critical_service_id, datacenter_zone, door_name)
    | eval detail=printf("door=%s result=%s", door_name, access_result)
    search index=environmental (sourcetype=sensor:temperature OR sourcetype=sensor:humidity) earliest=-7d threshold_status IN ("critical","alarm")
    | eval resilience_stream="environmental"
    | eval service_id=coalesce(critical_service_id, site_id, rack_row)
    | eval detail=printf("sensor=%s reading=%s", sensor_id, reading)
    search index=itsi_summary earliest=-7d (alert_value>threshold OR alert_color IN ("red","orange"))
    | eval resilience_stream="service_health"
    | eval service_id=serviceid
    | eval detail=printf("kpi=%s severity=%s", kpiid, alert_color)
]
| lookup critical_services.csv service_id OUTPUT service_name, owner, recovery_time_hours, recovery_point_hours
| stats latest(_time) as last_signal values(resilience_stream) as streams values(detail) as details count by service_id owner recovery_point_hours
| eval hours_since=round((now()-last_signal)/3600,2)
| where hours_since>recovery_point_hours OR isnull(owner)
| sort - hours_since
description: Correlates failed or stale backup jobs, physical-access anomalies in sensitive zones, breached environmental thresholds, and ITSI service-health breaches against the business service catalog so resilience gaps surface as one evidence stream.
value: Stops compliance discussions from resting on paper exercises alone by showing, in one place, whether recovery points, facility protections, and service health still look healthy enough to survive real outages.
implementation: Normalize vendor backup exports into `backup:job_status`; send access-control and BACnet/BMS sensor streams to `physical` and `environmental` indexes; accelerate ITSI KPI summaries. Maintain `critical_services.csv` with owners and recovery targets. Install Splunkbase 1841 and 263 where licensed. Schedule the multisearch daily; archive positive rows to `index=audit_evidence`.
visualization: Four-tile executive row (backup, physical, environmental, ITSI) plus a single merged table (service_key, streams, hours_since, owner); optional geomap if `site_id` carries coordinates.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts where nodename=Alerts.All_Alerts by Alerts.dest, Alerts.signature | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Alerts only if you add ES notables; ITSI already relies on itsi_summary acceleration jobs—leave defaults unless Support advises.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Planned generator load-test trips HVAC alarms** — Distinguish: Facilities team raises temporary humidity excursion during controlled test. Suppress: `nis2_facility_exception_register.csv` time-bound exception with ticket id.
• **Backup job "CompletedWithExceptions" for non-critical VM** — Distinguish: guest OS quiesce warning but chain still restorable. Suppress: filter SPL `where priority_tier="Tier1"` after lookup adds tier from `critical_services.csv`.
• **Contractor badge denied at exit reader twice** — Distinguish: benign mis-tap; no entry event. Suppress: require `access_result="Denied"` paired with `direction="inbound"` via `where` clause.
• **ITSI orange KPI during sanctioned maintenance** — Distinguish: maintenance window suppresses automation in vendor UI. Suppress: join `maintenance_calendar.csv` on `service_id` and `_time` as time-bound exception lookup.
• **Laggy itsi_summary replication in DR site** — Distinguish: events arrive **15 minutes** late but healthy in primary region. Suppress: raise `recovery_point_hours` threshold only for DR-specific `service_id` using a dedicated lookup column.
equipmentModels: Schneider Electric EcoStruxure / APC NetBotz (environmental sensor data via SNMP or HEC)
Splunk Add-on for Veeam Backup & Replication (Splunkbase 5601, backup job status)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (HEC for environmental sensor and generator telemetry)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, optional incident linkage)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `backup`, `physical`, `environmental`, `itsi_summary` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `critical_services.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('critical_services.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(c)`. The control objective is: "Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.21(2)(c) using NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical-environmental resilience evidence." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `backup`, `physical`, `environmental`, `itsi_summary` under `backup:job_status`, `access_control:door`, `sensor:temperature`, `sensor:humidity`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


One operational consideration shapes the deployment of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Normalize vendor backup exports into `backup:job_status`; send access-control and BACnet/BMS sensor streams to `physical` and `environmental` indexes; accelerate ITSI KPI summaries. Maintain `critical_services.csv` with owners and recovery targets. Install Splunkbase 1841 and 263 where licensed. Schedule the multisearch daily; archive positive rows to `index=audit_evidence`. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical-environmental resilience evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `critical_services.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`service_name, owner, recovery_time_hours, recovery_point_hours`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `service_id`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `critical_services.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval service_id = lower(service_id)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 7 days.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Alerts` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Alerts where nodename=Alerts.All_Alerts by Alerts.dest, Alerts.signature | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Alerts` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.53: NIS2 Art.21 business continuity, backup restore and physical]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.53,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `service_id`, `resilience_stream`, `last_signal`, `hours_since`, `owner`. Run:

```spl
index=backup sourcetype=backup:job_status earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table service_id, resilience_stream, last_signal, hours_since, owner
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `critical_services.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup critical_services.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.53: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Four-Tile Executive Row (Backup, Physical, Environmental, Itsi) Plus A Single Merged Table (Service Key, Streams, Hours Since, Owner); Optional Geomap If `Site Id` Carries Coordinates (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We bundle the boring-but-vital stuff—backups, building access, room climate, and whether key services are actually healthy—into one easy view. When any piece falls behind what leadership promised, we spot it early instead of discovering it mid-crisis.
